author: jsign
content:
Could finally read the article, very cool!

Some questions for <@745384946310578317> / <@976542403269910539> (numerating since might be easier to reply):
1. Is the process of building the final ELF that you used for benchmarks part of a transparent CI that we can take a look at?
2. Would be possible through that CI (if exists) you can put this ELF in releases in the `zilkworkm` repo? We would like to pull all official ELFs from team’s repos (as source of truth) and probably re-publish them in `ere-guests` for convenience. This would make `zkevm-benchmark-workload` not have to rely in custom URLs for downloading elfs.
3.  Do you have (or have plans for) supporting Zisk v0.18?
4. Do you have plans to accept also as untouched guest inputs the current EEST fixtures `statelessInputBytes`? We are trying to get everyone accepting this to have a 100% pure dataset run style to measure the performance for everyone — from that point forward we can learn lessons that you have experimented on how maybe change the specs in a way that can allow more efficient validation of witness or similar; but having exceptions on some guest programs having custom inputs is a bit complicated. Is at least having a separate variant of Zilkworm accepting current EEST `statelessInputBytes` as is possible? (You can still keep optimizing your other custom variant and hopefully at some point we can unify them)

cc <@944007601400741989> <@466102668138512394> for visibility

author: canepat
content:
Thank you!

Some answers on my side:
1. yes, it's part of the `release.yml` CI workflow on this branch: https://github.com/erigontech/zilkworm/tree/canepat/ere_benchmark_integration
2. it's already like that, this release of Zilkworm was produced by such workflow: https://github.com/erigontech/zilkworm/releases/tag/v0.1.0-alpha.2-ere

author: somnergy
content:
3. We do have plans for Zisk, OpenVM and Airbender (official public release), we might start some time next month subject to various factors like project continuation etc.
4. Right now it supports standard EEST JSON as-is (with a flag) and the custom input. Working on any other format would be an add-on, we might support it in future, again subject to priorities and project continuation etc.

Certainly the format(s) that should be fed to the guest vs the format for wire transport could and should be different. So, we may meet in the middle for one of these? 

We are introducing a multi-format support spec internally for zilkworm with support for Json, flat-single-block-witness, flat-multi-block witness and space for future expansion. We can readily plug in a new format in future with this standard. If you want to standardize things looking at the way we are doing it, you may.

I am trying to tune the format(s) to the program and still keeping it as generic as possible in doing so. It is experimental work, of course.

author: jsign
content:
> Certainly the format(s) that should be fed to the guest vs the format for wire transport could and should be different. So, we may meet in the middle for one of these? 

Ideally every ELF of all the guest programs to be used in the protocol can receive exactly the same bytes as inputs. The rationale for this is simplifying hte proving infrastructure such that you don't need a million libraries to adjust inputs for each guest program, to keep "running a prover" something that is doable for a mortal

For now the goal is that every guest program supports the `statelessInputBytes` from the specs (e.g. latest https://github.com/ethereum/execution-specs/releases/tag/tests-zkevm%40v0.4.1).

When you say:
>  EEST JSON as-is (with a flag)
means this `statelessInputBytes` field?

I don't know how much you have dived into what is that `statelessInputBytes` in the EEST fixtures, but is basically SSZ_encoded(new_payload_request + execution_witness + extra stuff) where `execution_witness` is roughly the usual `state`, `code` and `headers` .

I think we know prob the `state` current construction isn't optimal since for example sorts the RLP-bytes of MPT nodes lexicographically (just to make things canonical) -- and prob would be better to sort it in a way that allows a linear walk of the array as the MPT proofs are being checked as part of input validation, etc.

But as a first milestone it would be good that everyone can accept the same bytes and reduce hte complexity of having to create K proofs as much as possilbe without having customized inputs.
After that happens, we can keep iterating in improving these specs to optimize htei nput in any way that we think is more performant and actually benchmark that optimization idea improves performance in multiple ELFs (and not make decisions of "Yeah let's do X idea in the specs since we noticed at least helps Reth" -- since we need optimizations proved to be useful for most guest programs and not particular ones.

author: jsign
content: Maybe I extended a bit on my answer here, but want to explain better why we are doing the things we are doing. Ofc each guest can still support multiple formats to experiment on stuff and I think that's gaining time since we want to eventually pull into the spec good ideas on how to make something faster *for everyone* ideally using the same raw byte inputs. So if you can support temporarily multiple input bytes that's nice, since we can work on these things in parallel.

author: somnergy
content:
EEST JSON = the .json text file of an EEST Release published for usual EL clients, nothing to do with witness. Those EEST jsons do not require special state extraction.

I assumed other "guest" programs also could run this, but maybe not.
We may have different objectives, uniformity isn't necessarily a good thing aside from testing. Standardization in this case would be counter-productive. But we can add support as an extension.

author: somnergy
content:
We can have good ideas, the format can only benefit an implementation to a certain degree. Some quick wins like RLP -> SSZ is still within small margin of improvement (maybe less than 2%). But JSON -> RLP or SSZ is huge for everyone. These optimizations, i assume, are already part of all contending guest programs. The next set of improvements could be highly specialized and there is not much to do about that.
Like one could use key-path-based state representation, the other could use hash-based and yet another based on pre-determined flat layout to be used with a chain of pointers loaded serially. It's hard to find something that'll make everyone faster beyond basic
